text: The patient is positioned supine with legs spread, allowing the surgeon to stand between them. Three 12mm ports and two 5mm ports are inserted, with the possibility of an additional port, to facilitate the laparoscopic Whipple procedure.
The surgeon divides the fat between the colon and the lower border of the stomach, revealing the pink vascular tissue of the stomach's lower border.
The surgeon observes the continuation of the fat tissue from the lower border of the stomach extending down to join with the colon.
The surgeon mobilizes the colonic fat from the kidney fat on the right-hand side, separating these layers as part of the laparoscopic pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
The surgeon encounters adherent kidney fat to the liver in a slightly obese patient, visible during the laparoscopic pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
The colon becomes visible in the surgical field, appearing as a tubular structure with a distinct texture and coloration.
The surgeon mobilizes the right colon, carefully repositioning it to clear the surgical field for subsequent steps in the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues to divide the adipose tissue between the stomach and colon, gradually lifting the stomach away from the pancreas, which is visible behind the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a small opening behind the stomach, dividing additional fat tissue that extends over the duodenum.

The surgeon dissects the fatty tissue surrounding important veins draining from the stomach and a small artery running up to the stomach, carefully separating these vascular structures from the surrounding adipose tissue.
The surgeon continues the dissection behind the stomach, creating a visible tunnel underneath the stomach, which is now clearly demonstrated.
The surgeon demonstrates the view through the stomach, revealing the liver behind it. A prominent lymph node is visible over the hepatic artery, which runs up to the liver.
The surgeon exposes the pink pancreas behind the fatty tissue, continuing the dissection to remove the fatty leash containing vessels associated with the stomach.
The surgeon uses a stapler to transect the gastropoploic vessels as a whole, avoiding individual dissection of these vessels within the fatty leash.
The surgeon identifies a location for the division of the stomach, preparing to resect the duodenum as part of the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
The surgeon divides the stomach early in the operation to improve exposure of the pancreas, ensuring the stomach does not obstruct the surgical field.

The surgeon dissects around the prominent lymph node over the hepatic artery, carefully removing the node to expose the artery running up to the liver for preservation. The artery leading to the duodenum and pancreas is identified for division.
The surgeon continues dissection over and across the duodenum, mobilizing it from the porta hepatis, which is the entry to the liver.

The surgeon exposes the gastroduodenal artery, which runs to the pancreas and duodenum, preparing it for division. The hepatic artery is elevated using an instrument on the right side, while the portal vein is visible behind these structures, transporting blood from the bowel, pancreas, and spleen to the liver.
The surgeon performs additional dissection around the duodenum and pancreas, preparing for further division and mobilization of these structures.
The portal vein and common bile duct are visible in the surgical field, highlighting key structures that require careful consideration during the pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon carefully teases the bile duct away from the portal vein, separating these two structures during the dissection.
The surgeon passes an instrument behind the duodenum after completing the dissection to mobilize it from the porta hepatis.

The surgeon divides the gastroduodenal artery, which is visible running to the pancreas and duodenum, while the hepatic artery is held up with an instrument on the right side. The portal vein is visible behind these structures.

The surgeon divides the connection between the gallbladder and the barlet, providing more space for the procedure. The cystic artery, which is notably long in this case, is visible running to the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the barlet, ensuring efficient time management and preventing bleeding from the cut edge.
The surgeon approaches the pancreas from the opposite side, focusing on creating a tunnel underneath the pancreas at the intended division point.
The pancreatic neck is visible, running over the superior mesenteric vein, which transports blood from the bowel to the liver.
The surgeon lifts the pancreatic neck forward while teasing and dissecting the surrounding tissues. Small blood vessels are controlled during this process.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to seal small blood vessels while teasing the pancreas off the superior mesenteric vein. The vein is visible, bringing blood from the bowel to the liver, and transitions into the portal vein on the other side. The absence of branches in front of the vein allows for a safe tunnel creation.
The surgeon slowly creates a large window underneath the pancreas, allowing the instrument to pass through behind the pancreas to the opposite side, completing the tunnel formation.

The surgeon passes a tape around the neck of the pancreas, preparing to lift it up and forward to create tension on the tissues, facilitating further dissection alongside the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon opens a plane between the fat of the bowel, specifically the colon, and the head of the pancreas, including the uncinate process. This bloodless plane is adjacent to the vein, aiding in the separation of the colon from the pancreas.

The surgeon continues dissection along the plane separating the duodenum from the colon. The areolar tissue appears bubbly, indicating a safe area to divide without blood vessels. The duodenum is visible and being manipulated, while the surrounding colon fat is carefully teased away to facilitate further dissection.
The assistant uses a retractor to pull the bowel downward, revealing the previously prepared space between the colon and surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs a Cocker maneuver, rolling the duodenum away from the posterior abdominal structures. The inferior vena cava is visible as the operation proceeds in front of it.

The surgeon elevates the duodenum from the fatty structures of the bowel and the inferior vena cava, dividing the loose areolar tissue that attaches them. The dissection is bloodless due to the absence of blood vessels in this tissue.
The surgeon carefully pulls the duodenum from beneath the superior mesenteric artery and vein, along with the surrounding fatty tissue, revealing the aorta as it is pointed out.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the anterior surface of the inferior vena cava, progressing towards the liver visible in the background.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the vein running from the left kidney back to the inferior vena cava, which crosses the aorta just below the origin of the superior mesenteric artery. The vein is visible coming into view, with the superior mesenteric artery positioned just above it.

The duodenum is pushed to the right-hand side of the view, lying flat. Lymph nodes between the aorta and the inferior vena cava are being sampled and placed in a bag. The inferior vena cava, left renal vein, and aorta are visible.

The surgeon performs further mobilization along the left renal vein, while continuing to pull the duodenum through from the opposite side. A visible window has developed, allowing the duodenum to be seen from the other side.
The surgeon releases bloodless attachments around the duodenal jejunal flexure, also known as the ligament of Treitz, while managing the bowel on the opposite side.

The surgeon opens the bowel and divides it, creating an incision in the mesentery, which is the fatty tissue containing the bowel's blood supply. An energy device is used to seal and cut the tissue, allowing for precise dissection along the mesentery.

The surgeon has returned to the left side, with the duodenum pulled across. The head of the pancreas is visible above the instrument, surrounded by fatty tissue. A small vein runs from the head of the pancreas to the superior mesenteric vein, which can be divided. The bowel, now released from the other side, is pulled through, appearing purple in parts due to the loss of blood supply.

The surgeon observes the bowel turning pink, indicating restored blood flow, and divides it using a stapling device. The duodenum is pulled through, increasing mobility of the pancreas and duodenum. The structures are lifted away from the vessels, and a small tributary of the superior mesenteric vein is divided.
The surgeon dissects along the artery, carefully taking any branches running from it, including one large main branch and several smaller ones.

The surgeon lifts the uncinate process off the mesentery of the bowel, revealing the head of the pancreas elevated in the surgical field. The neck of the pancreas is divided at the point where it runs anterior to the visible vein.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully seal and cut through the vascular tissue of the pancreas, aiming to prevent bleeding from small blood vessels. Upon reaching the pancreatic duct, scissors are used to divide the duct, which appears as a pipe running through the pancreas. Clear pancreatic juice is observed flowing from the duct. The remaining pancreatic tissue is then divided using the harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon cuts a small piece from the edge of the pancreatic duct specimen, preparing it for laboratory analysis as a frozen section.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the artery and in front of the vein, carefully separating the pancreatic tissue from these vascular structures.

The surgeon clips and divides the branch of the inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery running up to the pancreas. Tributaries to the superior mesenteric vein and portal vein are similarly clipped and divided. The surgical field is cleared of lymphatic tissue behind the portal vein, with the sucker positioned under the hepatic artery, and the gastroduodenal artery clipped.

The surgeon retracts the vein using a sucker to access and remove the remaining lymph nodes. The inferior vena cava is visible at the back, along with the staple bile duct, gastroduodenal artery, and portal vein. The specimen is now completely removed.

The surgeon places the removed specimen into a plastic bag, preparing to enlarge one of the incisions for extraction later in the operation. The staple line is cut away, revealing a healthy blood supply to the bile duct, which is then sent for pathological examination.

A temporary clamp is placed across the blood supply of the bile duct to facilitate the joining process. The surgeon brings the bowel through the surgical field, ensuring it can comfortably reach both the bile duct and pancreas duct without tension.

The surgeon creates a small incision in the bowel, gently opening it with forceps in preparation for the anastomosis. The back wall of the bowel is stitched first, followed by the front wall, ensuring clear visibility of each stitch placement.

The surgeon carefully sews the bile duct and pancreas duct joins, completing the back wall suture and bringing it out in preparation for tying. The suture is not tied immediately but will be tied to the front wall stitch once it is brought through.

The assistant follows the stitch closely to maintain tension and prevent snags or loose sutures, ensuring a secure join. The surgeon completes the front wall stitching and ties it to the back wall stitch, finalizing the suture.
The surgeon begins the pancreas join, performing it in two layers. This step is critical due to the high risk of leakage, which can lead to infections.

The surgeon inserts a plastic tube or stent into the pancreas duct to aid in manipulating the pancreas during stitching, preventing accidental blockage of the duct by stitches. The pancreas appears highly vascular, with bleeding ceasing as the stitch is pulled through.

The surgeon performs a continuous suture along the back wall using a permanent stitch, creating a barrier. A small hole is opened in the bowel, similar to the bile duct join, and sutures are placed to secure the connection.

The surgeon performs the anastomosis using interrupted dissolving sutures on the pancreatic duct. Multiple sutures, approximately eight or nine, are placed due to the relatively large size of the duct. Some knots are tied internally, designed to dissolve quickly without causing interference.

The surgeon places a few sutures on the back wall and additional sutures at the corners of the anastomosis site. A stent is passed from the pancreatic duct into the bowel side to ensure a secure closure without narrowing the anastomosis.
The surgeon places front stitches on the pancreatic anastomosis without tying them, preparing for the next stage of the procedure.

The surgeon creates a small hole in the stomach and bowel to pass a stapler, which places staples on either side of a knife, forming a vertical line for the posterior join between the bowel and stomach. The visible white line indicates the completed join, while the hole at the front is subsequently closed.

The surgeon places a corner stitch on one side of the anastomosis, followed by a stitch from the opposite side. A new stitch with barbs is introduced, which locks in place without the need for knots, preventing loosening during placement.

The surgeon continues stitching along the staple line, ensuring the sutures include healthy stomach tissue beyond the staple line to maintain adequate blood supply. The stitching progresses towards the previously placed corner stitch, and once completed, the sutures are cut.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder, which was used as a handle to lift the liver during the bile duct anastomosis. The gallbladder is placed in a retrieval bag. Drains are positioned around the surgical joins to evacuate any residual blood or fluid, followed by irrigation of the abdominal cavity.
